what: Regression calibration, extended regression calibration, and Monte Carlo maximum likelihood
title: Confidence intervals
text: (extended) regression calibration Monte Carlo maximum likelihood, two types Wald intervals, obtained either transformation. transformation specified, wald.orig used obtain standard Wald intervals. transformation used, wald.transformed determined transforming, wald.orig obtained transforming using delta method (using transform.jacobian required). third option proflik, uses profile likelihood compute confidence bounds. , profile log (partial) likelihood parameter θp\theta_p defined PLp(θp*)=max𝛉:θp=θp*ℓ(𝛉),     PL_p (\theta_p^*) = \max_{\mathbf\theta: \theta_p = \theta_p^*} \ell (\mathbf \theta),  ℓ\ell log (partial) likelihood. Next, profile confidence intervals (θpl,θph)(\theta_p^l, \theta_p^h) obtained parameter θp\theta_p significance level α=0.95\alpha=0.95 solving −2{PLp(θp*)−ℓ(𝛉̂)}=χ1,1−α2-2 \{PL_p(\theta_p^*) - \ell(\hat{\mathbf{\theta}})\}=\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha} using bisection method, 𝛉̂\hat{\mathbf{\theta}} maximum likelihood estimate. Note profile likelihoods computationally intensive obtain. reason, ameras offers option determine exposure-related parameters, default setting. obtain profile likelihood intervals parameters, use params.profCI = "". illustrate, determine three types confidence intervals regression calibration analysis using example data, using linear excess relative risk model default exponential transformation (see Parameter transformations).

title: Fitting models and displaying output
text: Proportional hazards regression uses syntax, Y 0-1 status variable exit exit time variable. case left truncation, entry times can specified entry argument. Note BMA fits piecewise constant baseline hazard h0 proportional hazards model directly supported. default, observed time interval divided 10 intervals using quantiles observed event times among cases. number intervals can specified prophaz.numints.BMA argument. BMA output contains prophaz.numints.BMA+1 cutpoints defining intervals addition h0. , first fit models including quadratic exposure term setting deg=2. BMA output now contains intervals piecewise constant baseline hazards, corresponding estimates h0:  Without quadratic term (deg=1):

title: Relative risk models
text: non-Gaussian families, three relative risk models main exposure supported, usual exponential model RRi=exp(β1Di+β2Di2+𝐌iT𝛃m1Di+𝐌iT𝛃m2Di2),RR_i=\exp(\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2+ \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m1}D_i + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2} D_i^2), linear(-quadratic) excess relative risk (ERR) model RRi=1+β1Di+β2Di2+𝐌iT𝛃𝐦𝟏Di+𝐌iT𝛃m2Di2,RR_i= 1+\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta_{m1}}D_i + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2}D_i^2, linear-exponential model RRi=1+(β1+𝐌iT𝛃m1)Diexp{(β2+𝐌iT𝛃m2)Di}. RR_i= 1+(\beta_1 + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m1}) D_i \exp\{(\beta_2+  \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2})D_i\}.  vignette illustrates fitting three models using regression calibration logistic regression, syntax applies settings.

title: Relative risk models
text: usual exponential relative risk model given RRi=exp(β1Di+β2Di2+𝐌iT𝛃m1Di+𝐌iT𝛃m2Di2)RR_i=\exp(\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2+ \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m1}D_i + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2} D_i^2), quadratic effect modification terms optional (fit setting deg=1 passing anything M, respectively). model fit setting doseRRmod="EXP" follows:

code: transform1 <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params), lowlimit=rep(0,length(index.t)),                         boundcheck=FALSE, boundtol=1e-3, ...){      if(length(index.t)!=length(lowlimit))      stop("Length mismatch between index.t and lowlimit")   if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")   params[index.t] <- exp(params[index.t]) + lowlimit   if(boundcheck){     if(any(params[index.t]-lowlimit < boundtol))        warning(paste0("WARNING: one or multiple parameter estimates within ", boundtol, " of          lower bounds. Try different bounds or starting values."))   }   return(params) }  transform1.jacobian <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params), ...){    if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")   grad <- rep(1, length(params))   grad[index.t] <- exp(params[index.t])   if(length(params)>1){     return(diag(grad))   } else{     return(matrix(grad))   } }

title: Analyze Multiple Exposure Realizations in Association Studies
text: goal ameras provide user-friendly interface analyze association studies multiple replicates noisy exposure using variety methods. ameras supports continuous, count, binary, multinomial, right-censored time--event outcomes. binary outcomes, nested case-control design also accommodated. Besides common exponential relative risk model RR=exp(βD)RR=\exp(\beta D) exposure-outcome association noisy exposure DD, linear excess relative risk RR=1+βDRR=1+\beta D linear-exponential excess relative risk models RR=1+β1Dexp(β2D)RR=1+\beta_1 D \exp(\beta_2 D) can used.

title: Analyze multiple exposure realizations in association studies — ameras-package
text: Analyze association studies multiple realizations noisy uncertain exposure. can obtained e.g. two-dimensional Monte Carlo dosimetry system (Simon et al 2015 <doi:10.1667/RR13729.1>) characterize exposure uncertainty.     Methods include regression calibration (Carroll et al. 2006 doi:10.1201/9781420010138 ), extended     regression calibration (Little et al. 2023 doi:10.1038/s41598-023-42283-y ), Monte Carlo maximum     likelihood (Stayner et al. 2007 doi:10.1667/RR0677.1 ), frequentist model averaging (Kwon et al. 2023 doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0290498 ),     Bayesian model averaging (Kwon et al. 2016 doi:10.1002/sim.6635 ). Supported model families     Gaussian, binomial, multinomial, Poisson, proportional hazards, conditional logistic.

title: Analyze multiple exposure realizations — ameras
text: Fit regression models accounting exposure uncertainty using multiple Monte Carlo exposure realizations. Six outcome model families supported. first Gaussian family continuous outcomes, $$Y_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma^2),$$ \(\mu_i = \alpha_0 + \bm X_i^T \bm \alpha +\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2}D_i^2\). \(\bm X_i\) covariates, \(D_i\) exposure measurement error, \(\bm M_i\) binary effect modifiers. quadratic exposure terms effect modification optional. non-Gaussian families, three relative risk models main exposure supported, usual exponential \(RR_i=\exp(\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2+ \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2} D_i^2)\) linear excess relative risk (ERR) model \(RR_i= 1+\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2}D_i^2\), quadratic effect modification terms optional. Finally, linear-exponential relative risk model \(RR_i= 1+(\beta_1 + \bm{M}_i^T \bm{\beta}_{m1}) D_i \exp\{(\beta_2+  \bm{M}_i^T \bm{\beta}_{m2})D_i\}\) supported. second supported family logistic regression binary outcomes, probabilities $$p_i/(1-p_i)=RR_i\exp(\alpha_0+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha).$$ Third Poisson regression counts, $$Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_i),$$ \(\mu_i=RR_i \exp(\alpha_0 +\bm X_i^T \bm  \alpha)\times \text{offset}_i\) optional offset. Fourth proportional hazards regression time--event data, hazard function $$h(t) = h_0(t)RR_i\exp(\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha),$$ \(h_0\) baseline hazard. Fifth multinomial logistic regression categorical outcome \(Z>2\) outcome categories, last category referent category (.e., \(\alpha_{0,Z}=\bm \alpha_{Z}=\beta_{1,Z}=\beta_{2,Z}=\bm \beta_{m1,Z} = \bm \beta_{m2,Z}=0\)): $$P(Y_i=z)=RR_i\exp(\alpha_{0,z}+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha_{z})/\{1+\sum_{s=1}^{Z-1} RR_i\exp(\alpha_{0,s}+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha_{s})\}$$ Sixth conditional logistic regression matched case control data, $$P\left(Y_i = 1, Y_k = 0 \forall k \neq \bigg| \sum_{\\mathcal{R}} Y_i = 1\right) = RR_i\exp(\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha)/\{\sum_{k \\mathcal{R}}RR_k\exp(\bm X_k^T \bm \alpha)\},$$ \(\mathcal{R}\) matched set corresponding individual \(\). Methods include regression calibration (Carroll et al. 2006 doi:10.1201/9781420010138 ), extended regression calibration (Little et al. 2023 doi:10.1038/s41598-023-42283-y ), Monte Carlo maximum likelihood (Stayner et al. 2007 doi:10.1667/RR0677.1 ), frequentist model averaging (Kwon et al. 2023 doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0290498 ), Bayesian model averaging (Kwon et al. 2016 doi:10.1002/sim.6635 ).

BMA, prior distribution exposure-response parameters can chosen using linear linear-exponential exposure-response model. options normal, horshoe, double exponential priors, priors truncated 0 yield positive values. particular: Normal: \(\beta_j \sim N(0,1000)\) exposure-response parameters \(\beta_j\) Horseshoe (shrinkage prior): \(\tau \sim \text{Cauchy}(0,1)^+; \lambda_j \sim \text{Cauchy}(0,1)^+; \beta_j \sim N(0, \tau^2 \lambda_j^2)\). \(\tau\) shared across parameters Double exponential (shrinkage prior): \(\lambda_j \sim \text{Cauchy}(0,1)^+; \beta_j \sim \text{DoubleExponential}(0,\lambda_j)\) parameters, using exponential exposure-response model Gaussian outcome family, prior \(N(0, 1000)\). parameter \(\sigma\) Gaussian family, prior truncated 0. proportional hazards model available nimble, ameras uses piecewise constant baseline hazard Bayesian model averaging. interval min(entry), max(exit)) divided prophaz.numints.BMA subintervals cutpoints obtained quantiles distribution event times among cases, baseline hazard parameter estimated subinterval.
